retrieved_docs.title: New Onset Refractory Status Epilepticus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: An immune-mediated inflammatory process has been postulated as the underlying pathophysiology in FIRES. [16] In a study by Sakuma et al., the authors observed a marked overproduction of proinflammatory cytokines and chemokines in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) of children with FIRES, compared to CSF specimens from patients with other inflammatory neurological conditions. [17] The upregulation of these inflammatory mediators could be secondary to activation of T cells, glial cells, and cells of the blood-brain barrier and perhaps have a role in epileptogenesis. [18] However, additional mechanisms such as mitochondrial dysfunction or synaptic plasticity may also play a key role in seizure propagation. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Epilepsia Partialis Continua -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Studies on status epilepticus (SE) have shown a failure of gamma-amino-butyric acid (GABA)-ergic inhibition and consequent unimpeded action of N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) at the synaptic level to play a part in sustaining the epileptic activity. Scientists have postulated a similar process involved in EPC and how it is sustained over extended periods of time, but a causal relationship is yet to be established. [11] Studies have suggested that EPC and its evolution are likely to be related to autoimmune and inflammatory processes involving interleukin 1B and tumor necrosis factor-a. Neuronal cell loss, astrogliosis, and blood-brain barrier damage brought on by these cytokines may contribute to the underlying hyperexcitability of the cortex. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: New Onset Refractory Status Epilepticus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Recently, a personal predisposition towards developing FIRES or NORSE has been hypothesized based on allelic variations in human leukocyte antigen subtypes and IL1 pathway. [19] [20] The fact that FIRES is a subtype of NORSE, a similar autoimmune inflammatory mechanism has been considered as the most likely mechanism in patients with NORSE. Further research studies are required to understand the exact underlying pathophysiological mechanism in patients with NORSE and FIRES.

retrieved_docs.title: Landau-Kleffner Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: There were suggestions regarding possible implications of autoimmune factors in the genesis of some forms of epilepsy. [4] Other studies have shown that in children with LKS, an increased rate of autoantibodies directed against brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) is present. [5] Potential involvement of the immune system and/or an inflammatory dysregulation could justify the response to corticosteroids in most cases of LKS. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Seizure -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Everyone has some propensity to have seizures. The concept of a seizure threshold means that each individual exists on a seizure susceptibility continuum with many factors influencing that susceptibility. Medications, genetic factors, electrolyte abnormalities, sleep state, infections, brain inflammation, or injury from many causes may lead to an individual crossing that threshold with a resulting seizure.

retrieved_docs.title: Partial Epilepsy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The neuronal pathogenesis of epilepsy is not fully understood despite its high prevalence. Strong evidence suggests a genetic component affecting the transcription of specific principal neurons regulated by gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), an inhibitory neurotransmitter. Subtypes of principal and GABAergic interneurons are the most likely candidates for contributing to seizure triggering and propagation. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Receptors -- Clinical Significance -- Neurological Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: Signaling initiated by neurotransmitters binding to their receptors is crucial for neurotransmission and maintaining proper communication and function of neurons and glial cells. Dysregulation of these receptors can alter neurotransmission, synaptic plasticity, neuronal survival, and inflammatory responses, contributing to the manifestation of neurological symptoms and the development of neurological disorders. Examples are provided below.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Astrocytes -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Astrocytes also specialize in neuroprotection, neural repair mechanisms, and axonal growth promotion. Although astrocytes already defend the CNS by way of metabolic and homeostatic regulation, they also satisfy an immune function in the brain. Astrocytes can release and respond to both pro- and anti-inflammatory signals, such as cytokines and chemokines. Specifically, astrocytes release substances such as interleukin-6 (IL-6) and tissue necrosis factors (TNF) alpha and beta to promote immune responses in the brain. Astrocytes can also respond to inflammatory signals by undergoing a process known as astrogliosis. [5] [6] In astrogliosis, astrocytes become reactive and form fibrous scars that quarantine injured tissues, mitigating the spread of damage. [5] [6] Aside from preventing the incursion and spread of neural damage, astrocytes facilitate regeneration and repair of these damaged tissues. Though previous studies claimed that astrocyte scars impeded the regrowth of damaged axons, newer research has shown that astrocytes release axon growth-promoting factors such as nerve growth factor, brain-derived trophic factor, NT3, BDNF, and chondroitin sulfate proteoglycans 4 and 5 (CSPG-4 and CSPG-5) that are essential for axon growth both before and after injury. [6] [7] Also, astrocytes promote the formation and maturation of synapses by releasing factors such as thrombospondins (TSPs) 1 and 2 as well as glypican-4 and 6, respectively. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Corticobasal Degeneration -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Beyond tauopathy, the neurodegenerative cascade in CBD involves mitochondrial dysfunction, impaired axonal transport, inflammation, and synaptic degeneration. Neuroinflammation is a key driver, with activated microglia and astrocytes releasing proinflammatory cytokines, including tumor necrosis factor-alpha, interleukin (IL)-1 beta, IL-6, and complement proteins. Microglia also facilitate tau propagation through endocytosis and exocytosis cycles. [1] Elevated levels of chitinase-3-like protein 1, a marker of astroglial neuroinflammation, have been observed in CBD and other tauopathies. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Partial Epilepsy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic predisposition to epilepsy has also been attributed to an upregulation of the glutamate receptor genes, an excitatory neurotransmitter. As GABA becomes deregulated, the glutamate receptor initiates an uninhibited, unsynchronized neuronal activity that generates a seizure activity. Structural lesions such as tumors, sclerotic tissue, or damaged tissue will serve as epileptogenic areas due to the disruption of normal electrical propagation.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Glial Cells -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Astrocytes can form scars following injury as they hypertrophy or divide to fill spaces resulting from cavitation of neural tissue. Astrocytes also assist microglia in the regulation of the immune response to pathological insult in the nervous system by secreting interleukin-1 (IL-1) and prostaglandins, among other factors. Astrogliosis can cause the release of inhibitory molecules that prevent nervous system recovery. However, its impairment correlates with defects in the blood-brain barrier. While reactive astrocytes are the predominant cell type involved in glial scar formation, microglia, and endothelial cells and fibroblasts also play a role.

retrieved_docs.title: Idiopathic (Genetic) Generalized Epilepsy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The new classification emphasizes etiology as instrumental for diagnosis, prognostic counseling, and management of epilepsy. This is also vital in determining the recurrence risk of single seizures and for making a diagnosis of epilepsy versus seizure from a cause other than underlying brain disorder. [8] These groups can be overlapping in patients with more than one contributing etiology, and etiology should always be considered in conjunction with classification. Etiology is broken into six subgroups: [4] Structural Genetic Infectious Metabolic Immune Unknown

retrieved_docs.title: Focal Onset Seizure -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Specific clinical pathologies and conditions can predispose individuals to have seizures. Etiology should always be investigated alongside the type of focal seizure. According to the ILAE, the causes of seizures can be classified into the 6 categories below: [11] Genetic Structural Metabolic Immune Infectious Unknown

retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonic Epilepsy and Ragged Red Fibers -- Evaluation -- Laboratory evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The lactic acid levels are typically increased in both blood and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) in symptomatic as well as the asymptomatic patients with MERRF. The lactic acid is further increased after physical activity and exercise. CSF protein is also raised but often does not exceed 100mg/dl. A raised creatine kinase (CK) often demonstrates myopathy. [1] It is also important to exclude other treatable causes that may mimic mitochondrial disease. These include autoimmune inflammatory disorders, endocrinopathies (diabetes mellitus, adrenal, thyroid, and parathyroid disorders), vitamin B12 deficiency, and disorders of collagen formation. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Idiopathic (Genetic) Generalized Epilepsy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of epilepsy depends on the etiological factor causing the seizure activity. Recently, a study identified the regulatory effects of cortical regions of basal ganglia and cerebellum among IGE patients and established possible links between the neuroimaging findings and epileptic symptoms. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: New Onset Refractory Status Epilepticus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Anakinra, a recombinant version of the human Interleukin-1 receptor antagonist, has been observed to reduce the seizure frequency and achieve remission in two case reports. [40] [41]

retrieved_docs.content: be employed to treat the seizure condition described in Arabic as “نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة,” which falls under the broader categories of epilepsy and convulsions. Characteristic findings for epilepsy‑related convulsions are typically localized to the velký mozek (the cerebrum). Collectively, this class provides multiple mechanism‑specific options for individualized management of diverse epilepsy forms while emphasizing the importance of awareness of adverse effects, accurate data representation, and the neuroanatomical context of convulsive events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 868
retrieved_docs.content: Encephalitis, an inflammation of the brain, is situated in the organ referred to as “mozek” (the brain). The brain serves as the finding site for several neurological conditions, including اعتلال دماغي أيضي (a metabolic brain disorder), انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ (brain compression), and اعتلال دماغي سمي (a toxic brain disorder). In addition to being the anatomical substrate for disease, mozek is the location where the biological process of myšlení (thinking) takes place, and it is also linked to the emotional experience of الشعور بالذنب (feeling guilt). Recognizing that encephalitis and these various disorders, cognitive functions, and emotions all converge on the brain underscores its central role in both pathology and normal mental processes, which is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1314
retrieved_docs.content: toxins, are employed to safely stimulate protective immune responses, and xenon, an inert gas with anesthetic properties, influences neuronal activity and cerebral physiology. Collectively, these diverse entities serve as important qualifiers for describing and investigating physiological mechanisms, disease processes, and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2485
retrieved_docs.content: Other nervous system disorders represent a broad category of neurological conditions that includes central nervous system inflammation (التهاب الجهاز العصبي المركزي) as one of its parent-level manifestations. Within this overarching group, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (خثار الجيب الوريدي الدماغي) is identified as a more specific, narrower disorder, illustrating the hierarchical relationship between general nervous system disorders and their particular subtypes. This classification underscores how broader neurological categories encompass both inflammatory processes of the central nervous system and distinct vascular complications such as cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, facilitating clearer diagnostic pathways and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2404
retrieved_docs.content: Encephalitis is a condition that occurs in the brain (mozek). The brain (mozek) also serves as the anatomical site for several pathological findings, including metabolic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي أيضي), brain compression (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ), and toxic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي سمي). These relationships highlight that the brain is both the location of encephalitis and the locus where these specific brain injuries or dysfunctions are identified, underscoring the importance of evaluating the brain for multiple concurrent or related pathologies in clinical assessment.

Together, these interconnections emphasize the centrality of the brain in diverse pathological, diagnostic, and therapeutic contexts, and they underscore the importance of recognizing overlapping disease categories for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

Collectively, these relationships depict “Other disorders of nervous system” as an integrative framework that connects diverse neuropathic, vascular, inflammatory, toxic, traumatic, hereditary, and musculoskeletal entities to their anatomical sites, diagnostic findings (including abnormal brain evoked‑potential maps), and therapeutic interventions, thereby guiding clinicians in the comprehensive recognition and management of nervous‑system disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1079
retrieved_docs.content: Encephalitis is described as being located in the brain (mozek). The brain itself serves as the anatomical site for several pathological findings, including metabolic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي أيضي) and brain compression (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ). In addition, the brain is identified as the location of toxic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي سمي). Beyond structural pathology, the brain is also linked to the psychological process of feeling guilt (الشعور بالذنب), indicating its role in emotional and cognitive functions. Therapeutically, the brain is the direct procedural site for electroconvulsive therapy (العلاج بالتخليج الكهربي), a treatment that targets neural activity. Together, these relationships highlight the brain’s central involvement in infectious, metabolic, compressive, toxic, and emotional conditions, as well as its importance as a target for specific neuropsychiatric interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2397
retrieved_docs.content: The brain (mozek) is the anatomical location for a range of pathological findings and a key physiological process. Encephalitis is located in the brain. The brain serves as the finding site for a brain cyst (كيسة دماغية), a metabolic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي أيضي), brain compression (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ), and a toxic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي سمي). In addition, the brain is the site of the biological process of thinking (myšlení). This underscores the brain’s central role in both disease manifestation and cognitive function, highlighting its importance in medical evaluation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Zonisamid exerts its therapeutic effects, at least in part, through inhibition of P‑glycoprotein, making P‑glycoprotein inhibition a key mechanism of action of this antiepileptic medication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: CT scan of the head that reveals a periventricular cyst is classified as a كيسة دماغية (brain cyst), and the brain (mozek) is identified as the anatomical site for this cyst as well as for a range of other neuropathological findings, including اعتلال دماغي أيضي (metabolic brain disease), انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ (brain compression), أوديما دماغية (cerebral edema), اضطراب دماغي غير محدد (unspecified brain disorder), اعتلال دماغي (brain disease), and اعتلال دماغي سمي (toxic brain disease). Encephalitis is also localized to the brain, underscoring the organ’s central role in infectious and inflammatory processes. In addition to structural pathology, the brain is linked to the psychological process of الشعور بالذنب (guilt feeling), reflecting the organ’s involvement in affective neurobiology. Therapeutically, العلاج بالتخليج الكهربي (electro‑coagulation therapy) is performed directly on the brain, illustrating a direct interventional approach to cerebral lesions. Migraine with ischemic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 42
retrieved_docs.content: cerebrovascular disease is linked as a parent condition to persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction, highlighting the vascular nature of severe migraine-related brain injury. Together, these relationships illustrate how various imaging findings, inflammatory conditions, metabolic and toxic brain pathologies, neurological syndromes, and therapeutic interventions are interconnected through their common involvement of the mozek (brain) and related cerebrovascular and edema processes, underscoring the importance of integrated diagnostic and treatment strategies in neuro‑medical care.
